news_summary: Today's positive news highlights new insights into ancient human history through DNA discovery and a growing movement encouraging families to unplug from screens and reconnect.
news_summary_fr: Les bonnes nouvelles d'aujourd'hui mettent en lumière de nouvelles perspectives sur l'histoire de l'humanité grâce à des découvertes en matière d'ADN, ainsi qu'un mouvement de plus en plus important qui encourage les familles à se déconnecter des écrans et à renouer des liens.
news_summary_es: Las noticias positivas de hoy ponen de relieve nuevos hallazgos sobre la historia antigua de la humanidad gracias a los descubrimientos en materia de ADN, así como un movimiento cada vez más extendido que anima a las familias a desconectarse de las pantallas y volver a conectar entre sí.

articles.title: Britain’s 11,000-year-old “oldest northerner” was a 3-year-old girl, DNA reveals
articles.summary: Scientists have identified the oldest known human remains in Northern Britain as a young girl who lived around 11,000 years ago. Found in a Cumbrian cave and nicknamed the “Ossick Lass,” she was likely between 2.5 and 3.5 years old when she died. Nearby jewelry and evidence of multiple burials suggest the cave held deep spiritual importance for some of Britain’s earliest hunter-gatherers. The discovery is shedding new light on life — and death — just after the Ice Age.
articles.content:
The oldest human remains ever discovered in Northern Britain have now been identified as those of a young girl who lived around 11,000 years ago.

The remains were uncovered inside Heaning Wood Bone Cave near Great Urswick in Cumbria during excavations led by local archaeologist Martin Stables. The discovery provided some of the clearest evidence yet of Mesolithic burials in northern Britain.

Three years after the bones were found, an international team led by researchers from the University of Lancashire successfully extracted DNA from the remains. Their analysis showed the child was female and likely between 2.5 and 3.5 years old when she died.

"It is the first time we have been able to be so specific about the age of a child whose remains are so old and be certain that they are from a female," said lead researcher Dr. Rick Peterson.

One of Europe's Earliest Mesolithic Burials

Researchers say the burial is now considered the third oldest known Mesolithic burial in northwestern Europe. The remains also represent some of the earliest evidence of human activity in Britain after the last Ice Age ended.

Additional discoveries at the cave have strengthened the idea that the site was used intentionally for burial rituals. Archaeologists recently uncovered jewelry that included a pierced deer tooth and beads, all carbon dated to around 11,000 years ago.

"Dating the jewelry to the same time frame as the remains provides more evidence that this was a deliberate burial and opens up conversations about the significance of cave burials during this period," added Rick.

"Modern hunter-gatherer groups often see caves as a gateway into the spirit world, and this may be why we see so many caves used for burial by Early Mesolithic hunter-gatherers in northern Europe."

The Story Behind the "Ossick Lass"

Because Stables is from Great Urswick himself, he wanted the child's identity to remain connected to the village where she was buried for thousands of years. He chose the name "Ossick Lass," using local dialect meaning "Urswick girl."

Stables is entirely self-taught as an archaeologist and began excavating the site out of a personal fascination with the village's prehistoric past.

He said: "It's nearly 10 years since I started the excavations in July 2016 and I couldn't have envisaged the journey I have undertaken. It's as if I've went back in time to 9,000 BC.

"In doing so I traveled through the Bronze and Neolithic Ages, discovering some of those that lived then and what they left behind. Ultimately, reaching the Mesolithic Period has obviously become the highlight of the excavation and something that's particularly poignant to myself. Effectively, I was the first to bear witness to the obviously caring burial of someone's child that occurred over 11,000 years ago.

"The publication of this research paper is an important stage, that in due course, allows us to reveal further information about this unique site of national importance. My journey continues, but in the present as this is just the beginning and there is much more we plan to tell."

Rare Discovery in Northern Britain

Ancient human remains are far more commonly found in southern England and Wales. In northern Britain, evidence from this era is extremely rare because glaciers from the Ice Age heavily altered the landscape and destroyed many archaeological traces.

Before this discovery, the earliest known human remains in northern Britain came from a 10,000-year-old burial uncovered in nearby Kent's Bank Cavern in 2013.

Researchers also determined that at least eight individuals were buried in the Heaning Wood Bone Cave over several different prehistoric periods. Evidence suggests all of the burials were intentional.

The burials date to three separate time periods: around 4,000 years ago during the Early Bronze Age, about 5,500 years ago in the Early Neolithic period, and approximately 11,000 years ago during the earliest part of the Mesolithic era.

The research detailing the discovery and DNA findings was published in the Proceedings of the Prehistoric Society Journal.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant archaeological discovery with broad cultural and historical importance, shedding new light on early human life and burial practices in Northern Britain around 11,000 years ago. The findings provide meaningful insights into prehistoric human activity and spirituality, representing a rare and well-documented breakthrough in understanding ancient human history in this region.
articles.personality_title: DNA reveals Britain’s oldest northern human was a 3-year-old girl
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long searched for clues about the earliest people living in northern Britain after the Ice Age. Human remains from this time are rare in the north because glaciers changed the land and destroyed many signs of early life.

**What happened** – In a cave in Cumbria called Heaning Wood Bone Cave, archaeologists found the oldest human remains ever discovered in northern Britain. The bones belong to a young girl who lived about 11,000 years ago. Using DNA analysis, researchers confirmed she was between 2.5 and 3.5 years old when she died. Nearby, they also found ancient jewelry, suggesting the cave was an important burial site for early hunter-gatherers.

**Impact** – This discovery is special because it is one of the oldest known burials from the Mesolithic period in northwest Europe. It gives new details about how early people in Britain cared for their dead and used caves for spiritual reasons. The fact that the girl was carefully buried with jewelry shows that these early communities had complex traditions.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue studying the cave and other remains found there. They hope to learn more about the different people buried over thousands of years and understand their way of life better. This work is just the beginning of uncovering more about Britain’s ancient past.

**One-sentence takeaway** – DNA from a young girl’s bones found in a northern British cave reveals new insights into early human life and burial customs 11,000 years ago.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'ADN révèle que la plus ancienne habitante du nord de la Grande-Bretagne était une fillette de 3 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent depuis longtemps des indices sur les premières populations vivant dans le nord de la Grande-Bretagne après la dernière période glaciaire. Les restes humains de cette époque sont rares dans le nord car les glaciers ont modifié le paysage et détruit beaucoup de traces anciennes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Dans une grotte du Cumbria appelée Heaning Wood Bone Cave, des archéologues ont découvert les plus anciens restes humains jamais trouvés dans le nord de la Grande-Bretagne. Ces os appartiennent à une jeune fille ayant vécu il y a environ 11 000 ans. Grâce à l’analyse ADN, les chercheurs ont confirmé qu’elle avait entre 2,5 et 3,5 ans à sa mort. À proximité, ils ont aussi trouvé des bijoux anciens, ce qui suggère que la grotte était un lieu d’enterrement important pour les premiers chasseurs-cueilleurs.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est unique car elle fait partie des plus anciennes sépultures connues de la période mésolithique en Europe du nord-ouest. Elle apporte de nouveaux détails sur la façon dont les premiers habitants de Grande-Bretagne prenaient soin de leurs morts et utilisaient les grottes pour des raisons spirituelles. Le fait que la fillette ait été enterrée avec des bijoux montre que ces communautés anciennes avaient des traditions complexes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à étudier la grotte et les autres restes découverts. Ils espèrent en apprendre davantage sur les différentes personnes enterrées sur plusieurs milliers d’années et mieux comprendre leur mode de vie. Ce travail est seulement le début pour découvrir davantage sur le passé ancien de la Grande-Bretagne.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – L’ADN des os d’une fillette trouvée dans une grotte du nord de la Grande-Bretagne révèle de nouvelles informations sur la vie et les rites funéraires il y a 11 000 ans.
articles.personality_title_es: El ADN revela que la persona más antigua del norte de Gran Bretaña fue una niña de 3 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han buscado durante mucho tiempo pistas sobre las primeras personas que vivieron en el norte de Gran Bretaña después de la última Edad de Hielo. Los restos humanos de esa época son raros en el norte porque los glaciares cambiaron el paisaje y destruyeron muchas señales antiguas.

**Qué pasó** – En una cueva en Cumbria llamada Heaning Wood Bone Cave, arqueólogos encontraron los restos humanos más antiguos descubiertos en el norte de Gran Bretaña. Los huesos pertenecen a una niña que vivió hace unos 11,000 años. Mediante análisis de ADN, los investigadores confirmaron que tenía entre 2.5 y 3.5 años cuando murió. Cerca también hallaron joyas antiguas, lo que sugiere que la cueva era un lugar importante para entierros de los primeros cazadores-recolectores.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es especial porque es una de las sepulturas más antiguas conocidas del período Mesolítico en el noroeste de Europa. Ofrece nuevos detalles sobre cómo las primeras personas en Gran Bretaña cuidaban a sus muertos y usaban las cuevas con fines espirituales. El hecho de que la niña fuera enterrada con joyas muestra que estas comunidades antiguas tenían tradiciones complejas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores continuarán estudiando la cueva y otros restos encontrados allí. Esperan aprender más sobre las diferentes personas enterradas durante miles de años y entender mejor su forma de vida. Este trabajo es solo el comienzo para descubrir más sobre el antiguo pasado de Gran Bretaña.

**Resumen en una frase** – El ADN de los huesos de una niña encontrados en una cueva del norte de Gran Bretaña revela nuevas ideas sobre la vida y costumbres funerarias hace 11,000 años.
articles.image_prompt: A softly lit cave interior with delicate ancient jewelry—pierced deer tooth and beads—resting gently on a stone near a small, carefully arranged cluster of smooth bones shaped subtly like a child’s silhouette, surrounded by faint ethereal wisps suggesting a spiritual gateway, all rendered in warm earth tones and simple, natural colors.

articles.title: Ten days without screens: French families urged to unplug and refocus
articles.summary: Across France, children are being asked to put down tablets, switch off consoles and step away from their screens. The movement, called Dix Jours Sans Écrans, or Ten Days Without Screens, involves schools, nurseries and entire communities. But is it realistic? Delano D'Souza talks to digital parenting coach Elizabeth Milovidov about what parents and caregivers can do to make it through the 10 days.
articles.content:
One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site.

To display this content from YouTube, you must enable advertisement tracking and audience measurement.

Reading time 1 min

Across France, children are being asked to put down tablets, switch off consoles and step away from their screens. The movement, called Dix Jours Sans Écrans, or Ten Days Without Screens, involves schools, nurseries and entire communities. But is it realistic? Delano D'Souza talks to digital parenting coach Elizabeth Milovidov about what parents and caregivers can do to make it through the 10 days.

Screen use affects sleep, concentration and physical health as it leaves children more sedentary and it can slow down social and emotional development.

Elizabeth Milovidov, a digital parenting coach based in the UK, says the best activities are the simplest ones and recommends bringing back board games, arts and crafts and cooking.

She also says parents must not be afraid of letting children be bored. She gives parents and caregivers some great advice on how to balance modern technology and a happy family life.
articles.source: Health: news, videos, reports and analysis - France 24
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a meaningful community-wide initiative in France encouraging children to reduce screen time, which has positive implications for children's health, social development, and family well-being. It involves schools and communities, indicating broad impact, and provides practical advice, showing substance and focus on a single topic.
articles.personality_title: French families take part in 10-day challenge to reduce screen time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In France, many children spend a lot of time using tablets, video games, and other screens. This can affect their sleep, ability to focus, physical health, and how they get along with others.

**What happened** – A new movement called Dix Jours Sans Écrans, which means Ten Days Without Screens, is encouraging children across France to turn off their devices for ten days. Schools, nurseries, and communities are joining in to support this effort. Digital parenting coach Elizabeth Milovidov shared ideas to help families manage this challenge.

**Impact** – This is important because it helps children spend more time doing activities like playing board games, making art, and cooking. These simple activities can improve their social skills and health. It also reminds parents that it’s okay for kids to feel bored sometimes, which can encourage creativity.

**What's next step** – Families and schools will continue to support this screen-free time. The advice from experts like Elizabeth can help parents find a good balance between technology and family life even after the ten days.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A nationwide effort in France is helping children spend less time on screens and more time on activities that support their health and relationships.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des familles françaises relèvent le défi de dix jours sans écrans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En France, de nombreux enfants passent beaucoup de temps sur des tablettes, des jeux vidéo et d’autres écrans. Cela peut nuire à leur sommeil, à leur concentration, à leur santé physique et à leurs relations sociales.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un mouvement appelé Dix Jours Sans Écrans encourage les enfants à éteindre leurs appareils pendant dix jours. Les écoles, les crèches et les communautés participent pour soutenir cette initiative. La coach en parentalité numérique Elizabeth Milovidov a donné des conseils pour aider les familles.

**Impact** – Cela est important car cela pousse les enfants à faire des activités comme jouer à des jeux de société, faire des arts plastiques ou cuisiner. Ces activités simples améliorent leurs compétences sociales et leur santé. Cela rappelle aussi aux parents que l’ennui peut être bénéfique et stimuler la créativité.

**Prochaine étape** – Les familles et les écoles continueront de soutenir ces périodes sans écran. Les conseils d’experts comme Elizabeth aideront les parents à trouver un bon équilibre entre technologie et vie de famille même après ces dix jours.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une initiative nationale en France aide les enfants à réduire leur temps d’écran et à passer plus de temps à des activités qui favorisent leur santé et leurs relations.
articles.personality_title_es: Familias francesas participan en un reto de diez días sin pantallas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Francia, muchos niños pasan mucho tiempo usando tabletas, videojuegos y otras pantallas. Esto puede afectar su sueño, concentración, salud física y cómo se relacionan con otros.

**Qué pasó** – Un movimiento llamado Dix Jours Sans Écrans, que significa Diez Días Sin Pantallas, anima a los niños a apagar sus dispositivos durante diez días. Escuelas, guarderías y comunidades están apoyando esta iniciativa. La experta en crianza digital Elizabeth Milovidov compartió ideas para ayudar a las familias.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque ayuda a los niños a hacer actividades como jugar juegos de mesa, hacer manualidades y cocinar. Estas actividades simples pueden mejorar sus habilidades sociales y su salud. También recuerda a los padres que está bien que los niños se aburran a veces, lo que puede fomentar la creatividad.

**Próximo paso** – Las familias y las escuelas seguirán apoyando este tiempo sin pantallas. Los consejos de expertos como Elizabeth ayudarán a los padres a encontrar un buen equilibrio entre la tecnología y la vida familiar después de los diez días.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un esfuerzo nacional en Francia ayuda a los niños a pasar menos tiempo frente a pantallas y más tiempo en actividades que apoyan su salud y relaciones.
articles.image_prompt: A cozy family living room scene depicted in warm natural tones, where a wooden board game, colorful art supplies, and a simple homemade meal rest on a rustic table, while nearby a tablet and game console lie gently covered by a soft cloth, symbolizing a peaceful pause from screens and a joyful return to hands-on, shared activities.
